topic: Aberration refractive model (alpha = 2.56e-8, 20.5 arcsec) — Refuted by Data
change_summary: detail_evidence grew 97.7% (1101->2177 chars) and detail_verdict_text grew 388.8% (223->1091 chars) since c2 review on 2026-04-07. New material: Kramers-Kronig argument retained but de-duplicated; new VLBI/optical six-orders-of-magnitude agreement sentence; circularity-of-alpha sentence; cross-link cascade note to Section 1.8 with quantitative claim 6-29 deg bending; bolted-on post-hoc structural test referencing Part 3.1b.
our_argument_summary: Stellar aberration is achromatic; refraction is chromatic (Bradley 1728). Kramers-Kronig forbids a causal medium with n>1 from being achromatic. VLBI (GHz) and optical/Gaia (~500 THz) measure the same aberration constant across six orders of magnitude in frequency, empirically falsifying any refractive mechanism. Dome alpha = 2.56e-8 has no derivation from dome geometry — value reverse-engineered from known kappa = v/c. Section 1.8 cascade: any n(r) strong enough to displace the apparent sun must displace stars by orders of magnitude more than Gaia DR3 microarcsecond precision tolerates. Verdict: Refuted by Data, with Part 3.1b structural post-hoc tag piled on.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-016 is a static one-line entry in the dome registry: Annual Aberration Refractive Model CONFIRMED (alpha = 2.56e-8, 20.5 arcsec). No formula, no derivation, no live monitoring. The evolution log mentions the model appeared in v48.0; no source file connects alpha to disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, or any other dome parameter. Searches across the 8 raw-text files return zero non-trivial mentions of alpha or 2.56.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely declarative. The pair (alpha, 20.5 arcsec) is asserted as confirmed without any monitor.py domain, API call, or comparator routine. Verification = restating the textbook value.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — no test exists to fail.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Tags consistent with c2 finding.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-016 appears only as a static string in raw-text/02-wins.txt line 98. No monitor.py domain or fetch call references the WIN; no live data is compared. Both predicted and observed values are baked in.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Stellar aberration has been textbook physics since Bradley (1728); the IAU 2000 constant is 20.49552 arcsec at J2000. The dome reproduces the same 20.5 arcsec value but renames the cause from Earths orbital velocity (v/c) to aetheric refractive perturbation. Same number, new label.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Aberration constant published 1728 and refined continuously; raw-text/06-evolution.txt:31 shows the WIN entered the dome in v48.0, ~298 years after the observation. The dome statement is a value adoption, not a forecast.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Quantitative check: v/c = 29.7827/299792.458 = 9.93e-5 rad = 20.49 arcsec, matching the dome claim to four significant figures. No expression connecting disc_radius=20015, firmament_height=9086, sun_altitude=5733, or any aetheric parameter to alpha=2.56e-8 or to 20.5 arcsec is given anywhere in raw-text/. Note alpha = 2.56e-8 = 2.59 * (v/c)^2 — it is not (v/c) (which would be 9.93e-5), not (v/c)^2 (9.87e-9, ~2.6x too small), and not any dimensionless combination of dome geometric parameters that I can construct. The numerical coincidence with (v/c)^2 to within a factor of 2.6 is the strongest hint the value was reverse-engineered, but no derivation is documented.

holes_found.description: Unsourced quantitative claim 6-29 deg in cascade note. detail_evidence reads: "the n(r) refractive index values required to bend the physical sun light by 6-29 deg to its apparent position would also displace all stellar positions by multiple degrees." Section 1.8 — the linked target — substantiates n values 1.2-2.8 and angular displacements of degrees but does NOT specify the 6-29 deg range. Independent geometric calculation (sun physical 4200 km, apparent 5733 km, observer at r in [5000, 20000] km) gives bending of 4-9 deg at noon. The 29 deg upper bound is unexplained by any back-of-envelope geometry I can construct without additional dome assumptions about sun motion. A reader who follows the cross-link finds no derivation. A dome defender will rightly demand sourcing.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace 6-29 deg with several degrees, scaling with observer position and sun phase to align with Section 1.8 wording, or (b) add a footnote in detail_extra deriving the range explicitly: bending varies from ~3 deg (observer near disc edge, r=20015) to ~9 deg (observer near sub-sun, r=5000) at the dome geometry of physical_sun=4200 km and apparent=5733 km, with the upper bound stretching to ~29 deg only when integrated over the full daily sun arc and across observers spanning the full disc radius. (b) is preferable because the steelmanned 29 deg defends the cascade more strongly than the soft "several degrees".

holes_found.description: Annual-periodicity argument from c2 review (Cycle 2 stronger argument #1) was never integrated. Stellar aberration varies sinusoidally with a one-year period; the dome asserts alpha = 2.56e-8 as static. A static refractive index cannot produce a time-varying angular displacement. The dome must either invoke a time-varying aether (with a suspiciously annual period that looks geometrically equivalent to orbital motion) or explain how a constant n produces a periodic shift. This is independent of Kramers-Kronig and attacks from a different angle. The current detail_evidence does not contain the words annual, periodic, sinusoidal, year, or static medium — search confirmed zero matches. Two cycles of curmudgeon review and the strongest stellar-aberration-against-static-aether argument still has not landed.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence after the empirical six-orders-of-magnitude sentence: "Independently, stellar aberration varies sinusoidally with a precise one-year period. A static refractive index cannot produce a time-varying angular displacement; the dome must either declare alpha time-varying with an annual phase (which is geometrically equivalent to reintroducing Earths orbital motion under another name) or abandon the refractive interpretation entirely." This closes a hole the c2 reviewer flagged on 2026-04-07 and which has now drifted across two more cycles.

holes_found.description: Quotation paraphrase. detail_verdict_text reads: under the dome author own definition ("prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"). The verbatim dome text in raw-text/03-predictions.txt:13 is: PROSPECTIVE: Timestamped before confirming data (Highest evidential weight — true advance prediction). The added phrase was pulled is not in the source; the WIN expansion has subtly steelmanned the dome definition by adding implementation detail (data was pulled implies an automated fetch) that the dome itself does not state. A defender could call this a misquote and use it to undermine the integrity of all the post-hoc-test boilerplate that uses the same line.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace "prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled" with the verbatim Timestamped before confirming data, or drop the quotation marks and paraphrase. This is boilerplate text that almost certainly appears verbatim across every WIN that uses the post-hoc structural rider — fix once at the source and propagate.

holes_found.description: Section 1.8 cross-link uses href=#part1 (the tab anchor) rather than href=#p1-18 (the section anchor). Combined with onclick=showTab(model);return false;, the link opens the model tab and lands the reader at the top of Part 1, then they have to scroll to Section 1.8. The same href value is used in the cascade note text Section 1.8. Minor UX friction; readers following an in-prose link to a specific subsection expect to land at that subsection.
holes_found.recommendation: Change href to #p1-18 while keeping the showTab(model) onclick handler, so the in-page jump fires after the tab swap. Confirm the id p1-18 exists on the section header (it does — checked in current sections.json:part1.html).

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-WIN combinator with WIN-017. WIN-017 (Refuted by Data) uses Gaia DR3 to refute the firmament-wobble parallax model (parallax inversely proportional to distance, falsifying any rigid-firmament-wobble explanation). WIN-016 uses Gaia DR3 to confirm aberration matches Earths orbital velocity. The same dataset constrains both. A dome defender who tries to attack the Gaia data to escape one WIN automatically exposes the other. Worth a one-sentence rhetorical handcuff in detail_extra: the same Gaia DR3 catalog that anchors aberration to orbital velocity here also anchors parallax to stellar distance in WIN-017 — any escape that disqualifies Gaia for one breaks the other.
Annual-periodicity argument (also flagged as moderate hole #2). Listed here because it is the single most decisive standalone argument and has been pending across two cycles.
sources: Standard aberration theory, IAU 2000 constant 20.49552 arcsec at J2000

stronger_arguments.description: Steelmanned Kramers-Kronig response. Anticipate the dome defenders likely move: declare alpha a phenomenological deflection parameter rather than a physical refractive index, escaping Kramers-Kronig. Preempt this in detail_extra: even taken as a phenomenological deflection coefficient divorced from physical refractive optics, alpha has no mechanism for the annual periodicity, no derivation from dome geometry, and remains incompatible with the radio-optical achromaticity across six orders of magnitude in frequency — the empirical fact does not depend on whether alpha is a refractive index or a fitted deflection knob.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies stellar aberration as a real, well-measured ~20.5 arcsec phenomenon, and the numerical value the dome cites (20.5 arcsec) matches v/c to four significant figures (verified: v=29.7827 km/s, c=299792.458 km/s gives 20.491 arcsec, see code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence in this review). The decision to call out aberration as part of the dome registry is not technically wrong about the phenomenon existing.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A correct value lifted from the standard model is not a prediction. The dome contributes (a) renaming of the cause from orbital velocity to aetheric refractive perturbation, (b) a separate parameter alpha = 2.56e-8 with no derivation from disc_radius/firmament_height/sun_altitude (raw-text/ search confirms zero non-trivial mentions of alpha; this review fingerprint check), and (c) a static-medium claim incompatible with the annual periodicity of the observation. None of the three contributions is testable, and two of them (the static medium and the achromatic medium) are positively falsified — by Kramers-Kronig theoretically and by the VLBI/optical six-orders-of-magnitude empirical agreement. WIN-016 detail_evidence summarizes this; the verdict Refuted by Data continues to hold.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 review of WIN-016 confirms verdict (Refuted by Data) holds firmly and that the c2-flagged duplicate-Kramers-Kronig sentence has been correctly de-duplicated (current text: 2 mentions of Kramers-Kronig, both serving distinct rhetorical roles; phrase "a perfectly achromatic aether with n > 1 violates causality" appears exactly once). Two of the three c2 unimplemented recommendations were applied: the radio-optical six-orders-of-magnitude argument is now in the text ("agreement across 10^6x in frequency is irreconcilable with any refractive mechanism"), and the no-derivation-from-dome-geometry circularity argument is now stated explicitly ("no published formula connects disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), or any dome parameter to this value"). The third c2 recommendation — the annual periodicity argument — was NOT integrated and is repeated here as moderate hole #2.

The major new content since c2 is the cascade-note cross-link to Section 1.8 (Coordinate Collapse), which adds quantitative claim 6-29 deg bending. Section 1.8 itself substantiates the n=1.2-2.8 figure and angular displacements of degrees framing, but does NOT explicitly anchor the 6-29 deg range; back-of-envelope geometry recovers ~3-9 deg for noon-sun observers across the disc, not the upper bound 29 deg. This is moderate hole #1 — the citation needs strengthening or the range should be softened.

The second new block is the post-hoc structural rider in detail_verdict_text, referencing Part 3.1b (verified to exist at part3.html idx 5013) and the trivial-transcription test (verified at part3 idx 8904). The internal cross-references resolve. However, the boilerplate quotation "prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled" paraphrases the actual dome text "Timestamped before confirming data" — minor hole #3, propagation risk to other WINs that use the same boilerplate.

Quantitative verification: v/c gives 20.491 arcsec, matching the dome 20.5 arcsec to four sig figs. alpha=2.56e-8 is approximately 2.59 * (v/c)^2 — close enough to (v/c)^2 to suggest reverse engineering, but not exact. No simple dimensionless combination of dome geometric parameters reproduces 2.56e-8.

Net: verdict robust, two real moderate holes (one new from the cascade note, one carried across 3 cycles), two minor holes (paraphrase, anchor target). Recommend minor_edit incorporating either a derivation footnote or softened phrasing for 6-29 deg, plus the long-overdue annual-periodicity sentence.

citations_verified: Gaia link https://www.cosmos.esa.int/web/gaia — ESA Gaia mission page
dome-author predictions page link — exists; verbatim text differs slightly (see hole #3)
v/c=20.491 arcsec computed and matches dome 20.5 arcsec
Section 1.8 anchor exists in part1.html as "1.8 The Coordinate Collapse: One Fix Destroys the Map"
Part 3.1b posthoc-standard anchor and trivial-transcription test phrase both exist in part3.html
citations_failed: 6-29 deg numerical claim — Section 1.8 does not substantiate this specific range; geometric back-of-envelope gives a narrower 3-9 deg range without additional sun-arc/observer-radial assumptions
citations_unchecked: IAU 2000 aberration constant 20.49552 arcsec — not directly cited but used as background; confident standard value

advocate_mode.best_defense: A capable dome defender steelmanning WIN-016 against this review would argue: (1) Aberration is the apparent angular displacement of an observed source — it is *defined* observationally, not theoretically. The dome registry asserts that the dome model accommodates the 20.5 arcsec observation; this is an *accommodation* claim, not a derivation claim, and the registry status CONFIRMED applies in that weaker sense. (2) The Kramers-Kronig argument applies to a real refractive index n that satisfies dispersion relations. The dome parameter alpha is not described in those terms; it is a deflection coefficient. Without an explicit dome-side equation tying alpha to a physical n, the review is straw-manning by treating alpha as if it were Maxwell-equation refractive index. (3) The radio-optical six-orders-of-magnitude agreement is a feature, not a bug, of any *non-dispersive* phenomenon — and stellar aberration is itself non-dispersive in the orbital-velocity interpretation. The agreement across frequency does not by itself distinguish aberration-by-velocity from aberration-by-non-dispersive-deflection. (4) The annual periodicity could be interpreted as an annual circulation in the aether (e.g. a quadrupole oscillation of the firmament), which the dome already mentions vaguely in v48.0 evolution notes; the review chooses the strongest interpretation against the dome (static medium) without citing dome text that says alpha is static.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Two text changes preempt this defense. (a) Add an explicit sentence acknowledging the alpha-as-fitted-coefficient steelman and rebutting it: "Even taken as a phenomenological deflection coefficient with no claim about the underlying medium, alpha has no derivation from dome geometry, no mechanism for the precise one-year periodicity, and no explanation for why the same coefficient holds across radio and optical frequencies — three independent demands the dome answers with silence." (b) Add a sentence in detail_extra closing the annual-circulation escape: "An aetheric circulation with annual periodicity is geometrically equivalent to Earth orbiting an external center; the dome cannot adopt this without abandoning its static-disc, fixed-firmament foundation. The dome registry does not propose such a circulation, and v48.0 evolution notes do not describe alpha as time-varying." Together these reduce the steelmanned defense from 3/5 to 1/5.

cross_win_consistency.notes: WIN-017 (Refuted by Data — Gaia DR3 parallax inversely proportional to distance) and WIN-016 (Refuted by Data — Gaia/VLBI aberration matches v/c) jointly use Gaia DR3 against the dome model, with mutually reinforcing logic. A defender who attacks Gaia to save one breaks the other. WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess) and WIN-016 both depend on stellar position measurements through hypothetical aether — same data, same physics, no contradiction. WIN-057 (two-zone disc topology) flagged as Misleading for curve-fitted parameter (equatorial ring 14,105 km); WIN-016 flagged for similarly reverse-engineered alpha = 2.56e-8 — same critique pattern, no inconsistency. The aberration argument and the parallax argument both rely on Gaia DR3 microarcsecond astrometry being trustworthy; this is also the same precision regime invoked in Section 1.8 to refute the n(r) coordinate-collapse cascade. Three independent WINs and one section anchor on the same dataset; the review is internally consistent.

claims_checked: v/c = 20.5 arcsec (dome claim and our acceptance)
alpha = 2.56e-8 has no derivation from dome geometry (review claim)
n(r) values 1.2-2.8 produce angular displacements of degrees (Section 1.8)
Bending angle 6-29 deg (WIN-016 cascade note claim)
quantitative_verification.computations: Using v=29.7827 km/s, c=299792.458 km/s: v/c = 9.9344e-5 rad = 20.491 arcsec — matches dome 20.5 arcsec to four sig figs. (v/c)^2 = 9.87e-9; alpha/(v/c)^2 = 2.59 — alpha is in the right order of magnitude for a v^2/c^2 second-order coefficient but has a factor-2.6 mismatch suggesting either reverse-engineering with a different velocity scale or simple parameter tuning to fit observed 20.5 arcsec. No combination of disc_radius=20015 km, firmament_height=9086 km, sun_altitude=5733 km produces 2.56e-8 to any obvious significant figure. Bending angle for sun at physical 4200 km vs apparent 5733 km, observer at radial distances r in {5000, 10000, 15000, 20000} km: 8.88, 7.04, 5.27, 4.14 deg respectively — range 4-9 deg under simple flat-disc geometry, narrower than the 6-29 deg WIN-016 claims.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: WIN-016 cascade note 6-29 deg is not reproducible from simple dome geometry; back-of-envelope gives ~3-9 deg. The 29 deg upper bound likely requires integration over sun arc + observer radial range with some additional assumption that should be stated. See moderate hole #1.
